title: SUPER IS GRADED A FAILURE
full_text:
The nation’s biggest super fund is still failing to meet even the most basic customer needs a year after a spate of reputation-bruising scandals, a new study has found.
A staggering 90 per cent of customer calls to mega fund AustralianSuper are going unanswered, according to lobby group Super Consumers Australia.
This was the finding from a 10-week study of customer service standards across 20 major super funds including Australian Retirement Trust, Aware Super, CBUS, HESTA and Colonial First State.
Super Consumers gave the overall industry a failing grade and called for mandatory service standards.
The lobby group called out the $410bn AustralianSuper as the worst of the lot after it failed to answer 90 per cent of inbound calls to its call centre. Team Super came in second from the bottom, failing to answer half of all calls. AustralianSuper hit out at the findings, saying the study, done between August and October last year, took place as it was transitioning to a new call centre provider.
“Our customer satisfaction scores right now are the highest they’ve ever been, and our average speed of answer is less than two minutes,” the fund said.
AustralianSuper manages the retirement savings of about 3.6 million Australians and has been embroiled in scandal after scandal in recent years, including death benefit delays, charging members duplicate fees and systems that allowed hackers to steal $750,000 of member money.
While the megafund fared worst in the study, no fund scored strongly on meeting members’ needs, with close to a quarter of members told to “go online” when they called up asking for help.
“Superannuation is mandatory, but good customer service is not. That has to change,” Super Consumers Australia CEO Xavier O’Halloran said.
“This pilot study shows that, right now, getting the right support can depend on who answers the phone.” The findings come as financial advisers steer billions of dollars away from industry funds in favour of retail platforms such as Netwealth and Hub24, in the biggest shake-up of the industry in a generation.
Australia’s biggest industry funds, AustralianSuper, Australian Retirement Trust and Aware Super, all suffered outflows in the 2025 financial year as members exited these in favour of other stewards for their wealth.
AustralianSuper saw more than $250m in net withdrawals last year, as more members ditched the fund than joined. This was a sharp turnaround from recent years, in which it had strong flows from winning new members. At the heart of the issue is poor customer service and a failure to engage members before they seek out financial advisers as they approach retirement.
The Telegraph-20260714-Telegraph-22-000502657991
